{{Chess Opening Theory/Position|= |Sicilian, Alapin| |rd|nd|bd|qd|kd|bd| |rd|= |pd|pd| |pd|pd|pd|pd|pd|= | | | | | |nd| | |= | | |pd| | | | | |= | | | | |pl| | | |= | | |pl| | | | | |= |pl|pl| |pl| |pl|pl|pl|= |rl|nl|bl|ql|kl|bl|nl|rl|= }} =Sicilian, Alapin= This move continues development for Black. It puts pressure on the e pawn, and aims to protect the d5 square. This variation is marginally preferred to the 2...d5 response, and is statistically a better win-rate for Black (as per ChessBase.com). ==White’s Responses== 3.[[/3. e5|e5]] immediately pressures the Black knight, and is by far the most popular response. It also continues White’s assault on the centre, and aims to reinforce with d4. 3.[[/3. d3|d3]] and 3.[[/3. d4|d4]] are playable, but more passive. d3 aims to protect the e pawn, while d4 simply continues with White’s original plan of controlling the d4 square - the goal of the Alapin opening. ==Theory table== {{Chess Opening Theory/Table}}. :'''1.e4 c5 2.c3 Nf6''' {| border="0" cellspacing="0" cellpadding="4" |- ! ! align="left" | 3 |- ! align="right" | Sicilian, Alapin | [[/3. e5|e5]]
